title: About
text:
About
I am a computational biologist with experience in RNA-seq, single-cell analysis, and bioinformatics pipeline development.
My work focuses on reproducibility, scalability, and making complex biological data more interpretable.

title: NGS Figure Generator
text: The NGS Figure Generator is an interactive tool for transforming high-throughput sequencing outputs into publication-ready figures. It enables users to generate, customize, and refine visualizations to clearly communicate key biological insights.

title: NGS Figure Generator
text: The NGS Figure Generator is an interactive tool for transforming high-throughput sequencing outputs into publication-ready figures. It enables users to generate, customize, and refine visualizations to clearly communicate key biological insights.

title: NGS Figure Generator
text:
Problem
High-throughput sequencing workflows often produce complex outputs that require extensive post-processing to visualize effectively.
Researchers frequently:

spend significant time manually formatting figures for publication

rely on static plots that are difficult to iteratively refine

lack intuitive tools for exploring and highlighting key signals

This creates a gap between data generation and effective scientific communication.

title: NGS Figure Generator
text:
Approach
I developed this tool to streamline the transition from processed sequencing data to publication-quality figures.
The design emphasizes:

rapid generation of standard bioinformatics plots

interactive adjustment of visualization parameters

reproducible workflows for consistent figure generation

title: NGS Figure Generator
text:
Key Features

Interactive figure generation

dynamically adjust thresholds and visualization parameters

Multiple visualization types

support for volcano plots and heatmaps for expression analysis

Customizable outputs

refine annotations, clustering, and visual emphasis

Publication-ready formatting

generate clean, structured figures suitable for manuscripts

Extensible architecture

adaptable to multiple sequencing-based analyses

title: NGS Figure Generator
text:
Example Use Cases

Differential expression visualization (e.g., volcano plots)

Expression pattern analysis across samples (heatmaps)

Exploratory analysis of gene-level signals

Comparative visualization across experimental conditions

title: NGS Figure Generator
text:
Example Output

Differential Expression Visualization - Volcano Plot

This example demonstrates a volcano plot generated from differential expression analysis. Users can adjust statistical thresholds and highlighting to emphasize biologically meaningful signals.



Interactive Volcano Plot
This interactive volcano plot demonstrates how users can explore differential expression results and dynamically identify significant genes.


                            
                                            



Users can hover over points to view gene-level details and explore patterns in real time.




Expression Heatmap

This heatmap visualizes gene expression patterns across samples, enabling identification of clustering patterns and condition-specific signals. Users can refine clustering parameters and visualization settings to highlight key biological trends.



                            
                                            





Customizable Figure Refinement
The tool enables iterative refinement of figures, allowing users to modify parameters such as thresholds, clustering behavior, labeling, and visual emphasis to produce publication-ready outputs.

title: NGS Figure Generator
text:
Technical Implementation
The NGS Figure Generator is built as a modular web-based application that integrates data processing, visualization, and user interaction.

Frontend

Framework: React (with component libraries such as shadcn/ui)
Provides a user-friendly interface for:

uploading sequencing data (CSV/TXT)
selecting visualization parameters
customizing figure outputs in real time




Backend

Language: Python
Frameworks: Flask or FastAPI
Handles:

data ingestion and preprocessing
computation of analytical outputs
generation of visualizations




Data Processing & Visualization

Data handling: pandas

Statistical and ML methods: scikit-learn (e.g., PCA)

Visualization libraries:

Matplotlib / Seaborn → static, publication-ready figures

Plotly → interactive visualizations




Supported Visualizations

Volcano & MA plots: Matplotlib

Heatmaps: Seaborn / Matplotlib

PCA: scikit-learn (computation) + Plotly (interactive rendering)



File Handling

Supports structured input formats (CSV, TXT)
Designed for integration with standard bioinformatics outputs



Deployment & Reproducibility

Containerized using Docker for consistent environments

Deployable as a local server-based application

title: NGS Figure Generator
text:
Future Development
Planned enhancements include:

support for user-uploaded datasets

additional plot types (PCA, heatmaps, enrichment plots)

enhanced export options for publication workflows

title: ONT Methylation Pipeline
text:
An end-to-end bioinformatics pipeline for detecting and summarizing DNA methylation from Oxford Nanopore sequencing data. The workflow converts raw signal data into interpretable genomic methylation patterns through a structured and reproducible analysis pipeline.




Oxford Nanopore sequencing enables direct detection of DNA modifications, but transforming raw signal data into biologically meaningful insights requires coordination across multiple tools and processing steps.
Key challenges include: - converting raw signal data into high-quality basecalled reads
- extracting methylation signals at scale
- integrating heterogeneous tools into a cohesive workflow
- summarizing methylation patterns across genomic regions




I developed a modular pipeline that processes Nanopore data from raw POD5 files through methylation aggregation.
The workflow integrates established tools within a Nextflow framework, enabling reproducible execution, scalability across datasets, and clear separation of processing stages.









ont-methylation pipeline - Structured workflow from raw Nanopore signal data to aggregated methylation output.
Key stages:

Basecalling: POD5 → BAM using Dorado

Indexing: BAM indexing with SAMtools for efficient downstream access

Quality Control: assessment of alignment and read-level metrics

Methylation Extraction: per-base modification calls using Modkit pileup

Genome Integration: incorporation of chromosome sizes and genomic window definitions

Aggregation: summarization of methylation signals across defined genomic regions






End-to-end processing

from raw Nanopore signal data to region-level methylation summaries

Modular and extensible design

pipeline components can be executed independently or extended for additional analyses

Reproducibility

containerized execution ensures consistent environments across runs

Scalability

designed to handle large Nanopore sequencing datasets







BAM files containing basecalled reads

per-base methylation calls

aggregated methylation values across genomic windows








Nextflow-based pipeline orchestration





Dorado (basecalling)

SAMtools (indexing and QC)

Modkit (methylation extraction)





generation of genomic windows

aggregation of methylation signals across regions





Docker-based containerization for consistent execution environments





The pipeline leverages Nextflow parallelization to efficiently process large Nanopore datasets, enabling scalable execution across multiple samples and compute environments.







genome-wide methylation profiling

comparative epigenetic analysis

integration with transcriptomic or genomic datasets





https://github.com/fraserclaire/ont-methylation

Phylogenetic and evolutionary analysis of SARS-CoV-2.

title: SARS-CoV-2 Evolutionary Analysis
text:
An evolutionary genomics analysis of SARS-CoV-2 aimed at characterizing viral diversity, transmission dynamics, and evolutionary pressures across populations.
This analysis provides insight into how viral evolution is shaped by both transmission dynamics and selective pressures.




Understanding viral evolution is critical for tracking transmission, identifying emerging variants, and interpreting functional changes in the genome.
Challenges include:

managing and aligning large numbers of viral genomes

identifying meaningful sequence variation

reconstructing evolutionary relationships

interpreting mutation patterns in a biological context





I performed a comparative genomic, phylogenetic, and selection-based analysis of SARS-CoV-2 sequences to characterize temporal dynamics, geographic variation, and evolutionary pressures across variants.
The workflow includes sequence preprocessing, alignment, phylogenetic tree construction, and downstream analysis of evolutionary patterns.





Sequence collection and preprocessing

curated viral genome sequences

Multiple sequence alignment

alignment of genomes for comparative analysis

Phylogenetic reconstruction

inference of evolutionary relationships between samples

Selection analysis

identification of sites under evolutionary pressure across the viral genome






To characterize viral dynamics, I examined temporal trends, geographic variation, and phylogenetic structure across SARS-CoV-2 variants.









Temporal dynamics of SARS-CoV-2 cases and estimated reproduction number (Rt), including variant-specific trends.
This figure illustrates how case counts and transmission dynamics evolve over time, highlighting differences in transmission dynamics between variants and periods of increased spread.









Kernel density estimates comparing variant distributions between Hawaii County and Los Angeles County.
This analysis highlights geographic differences in variant prevalence, indicating distinct transmission dynamics and regional differences in variant prevalence.









Phylogenetic trees of SARS-CoV-2 variants, with samples colored by geographic origin.
The phylogenies reveal differences in population structure: - one variant shows intermixing across regions, suggesting high gene flow
- another shows clear separation, indicating more localized transmission
These patterns provide insight into how variants spread and evolve across populations.




To further characterize evolutionary pressures, I performed selection analyses to identify sites under positive or purifying selection across SARS-CoV-2 proteins.








Number of sites under selection across key protein-coding regions (e.g., ORF1a, Spike, ORF7a).
This analysis highlights differences in selective pressure across genomic regions, with certain proteins exhibiting higher concentrations of sites under selection, suggesting differential functional constraints and potential adaptive evolution across genomic regions.









Heatmap of sites under selection across geographic locations and epidemiological phases (exponential vs waning).
This visualization reveals patterns of shared and unique selection signals, indicating that selective pressures vary across populations and epidemiological phases, with both shared and region-specific signals of selection.








sequence alignment tools
phylogenetic analysis (BEAST, Mesquite)

selection analysis (FUBAR, FEL, MEME)

downstream analysis and visualization in R/Python





sequence filtering and formatting

alignment refinement

extraction of variant positions







variant-specific differences in transmission dynamics over time

regional variation in variant prevalence between populations

contrasting phylogenetic structures indicating both widespread mixing and localized transmission

evidence of selection acting on specific protein-coding regions, with variation across locations and epidemiological phases






viral surveillance and variant tracking

evolutionary analysis of pathogens

comparative genomics studies





https://github.com/fraserclaire/SARS-CoV-2_evolutionaryAnalysis
